name: George Coleman
abstract: George Edward Coleman (born March 8, 1935) is an American jazz saxophonist known for his work with Miles Davis and Herbie Hancock in the 1960s. In 2015, he was named an NEA Jazz Master.

concerts.label: Clark Terry and His Big Band

about_quotes.speaker: Nicki Parrott
about_quotes.evidence: on Thursday and Friday I did lunchtimes with George Coleman Junior on drums and Gloria Coleman on piano, at the Film Center in New York.
about_quotes.doc_title: Nicki Parrott interviewed by Monk Rowe. Utica, New York, November 6, 2010 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Cobb
about_quotes.evidence: I was in the last part of the band, where it was Ron Carter, George Coleman, Harold Mabern, and an alto player.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Cobb — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Harold Ousley
about_quotes.evidence: We had a lot of wonderful musicians in that band, George Coleman, the other saxophonist, and me.
about_quotes.doc_title: Harold Ousley interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 12, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Michael Weiss
about_quotes.evidence: Every time I get on the bandstand with George Coleman I’m going to learn a new song.
about_quotes.doc_title: Michael Weiss interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, December 22, 2021

about_quotes.speaker: Paquito D'Rivera
about_quotes.evidence: I was in the army listening very quietly... listening to Willis Conover the voice of America Jazz hour. ‘Ladies and Gentlemen, tonight we present the new Miles Davis Quintet with George Coleman and Herbie Hancock,’ blah, blah, blah.
about_quotes.doc_title: Paquito D'Rivera — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Elvin Jones
about_quotes.evidence: this one has Joe Farrell and also George Coleman and Wilbur Little on bass... I was so shy I said, 'I am writing music!' Everyone called him Elvin. I call him Mr. Jones... That’s was because we did Elvin’s record
about_quotes.doc_title: Elvin Jones — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
